title: Mapungubwe: An African Kingdom Long Ago
subject: History
caps_topic: An African kingdom long ago in southern Africa: Mapungubwe

meta.title: Mapungubwe: An African Kingdom Long Ago

meta.caps_topic: An African kingdom long ago in southern Africa: Mapungubwe

sources.title: Greater Mapungubwe Heritage Site and Tour

slides.heading: Mapungubwe: An African Kingdom Long Ago
slides.lead: About 800 years ago, southern Africa's first great kingdom grew rich from trade in gold and ivory - right here on our own land.

image.alt: A flat-topped sandstone hill glowing gold under an African sun, with a river below.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='250' cy='46' r='24' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M0 120 h320 v60 h-320 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M70 120 q10 -46 74 -46 q64 0 74 46 Z' fill='#c8934a'/><rect x='108' y='66' width='72' height='10' fill='#a8762f'/><path d='M0 150 q80 -14 160 0 q80 14 160 0 v30 h-320 Z' fill='#6a86a8'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by asking learners what they already know about kingdoms in Africa before Europeans arrived. Make the key point up front: long before 1652, southern Africans built a wealthy, well-organised kingdom that traded with faraway lands. 'Mapungubwe' is often said to mean 'hill of the jackal'. Today it lies in Limpopo Province.

slides.heading: A kingdom where three countries meet
slides.lead: Mapungubwe grew up in the Limpopo Valley about 900 years ago.

blocks.text: Mapungubwe is in the north of **Limpopo Province**, close to where the **Limpopo and Shashe rivers** meet. This is also where South Africa, Zimbabwe and Botswana touch today.

items: People first settled here in the early 1100s (about 900 years ago).
The kingdom was at its richest and strongest from about 1220 to 1300.
It was southern Africa's first kingdom - a place ruled by a king.

image.alt: A simple map showing two rivers joining, with a hill marked at the meeting point.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M20 40 q60 30 96 70 q30 30 100 42' fill='none' stroke='#6a86a8' stroke-width='7' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M60 20 q30 40 56 90' fill='none' stroke='#6a86a8' stroke-width='7' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M104 96 q10 -18 26 -18 q16 0 26 18 Z' fill='#c8934a'/><circle cx='130' cy='96' r='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/><text x='138' y='94' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='11' fill='#333'>Mapungubwe</text></svg>

slides.notes: Point out the location on a class map of South Africa - the far north, near Musina (Messina). Rivers mattered because they gave water for people, crops and cattle in a dry area. Stress the timescale for learners: 'about 900 years ago' is roughly 30 grandparents back.

slides.heading: A city on a hill
slides.lead: As the kingdom grew, the most important people moved to the top of a tall, flat hill.

blocks.text: At first people lived on the flat land below. As Mapungubwe grew rich and crowded, the **king and royal family moved to the top of the hill**, while most people lived on the plain below. Workers even carried tons of soil up the steep hill to make gardens and floors.

items: The hill has steep sides and only narrow paths to the top.
Living high up showed that the king was powerful and special.
Thousands of people lived in and around the town at its busiest.

image.alt: A tall flat-topped hill with small round huts on the summit and more huts on the plain below.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='120' width='240' height='40' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M70 120 q6 -60 50 -60 q44 0 50 60 Z' fill='#c8934a'/><rect x='86' y='58' width='68' height='6' fill='#a8762f'/><g fill='#7a5a3a'><circle cx='104' cy='52' r='7'/><circle cx='120' cy='50' r='7'/><circle cx='136' cy='52' r='7'/></g><g fill='#8a6a44'><circle cx='30' cy='132' r='8'/><circle cx='52' cy='136' r='8'/><circle cx='190' cy='134' r='8'/><circle cx='212' cy='130' r='8'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This is the heart of the social-structure story and comes back later. The move to the hilltop was a big change: for the first time in the region, leaders lived apart from and above ordinary people. Explain 'royal family' simply - the king and his relatives. The carried soil shows how much work people did to honour the king.

slides.heading: Farmers, herders and traders
slides.lead: Mapungubwe's wealth started with food and cattle, not just gold.

blocks.heading: Farming
blocks.text: People grew crops like sorghum and millet and stored extra grain for hard times.

blocks.heading: Cattle
blocks.text: Herds of cattle were a sign of wealth and were used to show how rich a family was.

blocks.heading: Crafts
blocks.text: Skilled workers made pottery, cloth, iron tools and beautiful gold objects.

slides.notes: Correct a common misunderstanding: the kingdom was not rich only because of gold. Good farming and large cattle herds gave people enough food and free time to trade and make crafts. Extra stored grain meant the community could survive dry years and support leaders and craftworkers.

slides.heading: Trading with faraway lands
slides.lead: Mapungubwe sold gold and ivory and bought beautiful things from across the sea.

blocks.text: Traders carried **gold and ivory** (elephant tusks) from Mapungubwe to ports on the east coast of Africa. From there the goods travelled by ship to lands far away. In return, Mapungubwe received glass beads, cloth and other treasures.

items: Mapungubwe traded with places as far away as China, India and Arabia.
Gold and ivory were carried out; glass beads and cloth came in.
This makes Mapungubwe part of world trade about 800 years ago.

image.alt: An elephant tusk and gold nuggets on one side and a string of glass beads on the other, joined by arrows.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 160' role='img'><rect width='260' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M28 96 q30 -34 60 -8 q-24 8 -60 8 Z' fill='#efe7d2' stroke='#c8b89a' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='40' cy='118' r='8' fill='#e8a13a'/><circle cx='56' cy='124' r='6' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M104 78 h52 M104 92 h52' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4' marker-end='url(#a)'/><g><line x1='104' y1='78' x2='150' y2='78' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M150 72 l12 6 l-12 6 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><line x1='156' y1='92' x2='110' y2='92' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M110 86 l-12 6 l12 6 Z' fill='#2f5233'/></g><g fill='#3f6bb0'><circle cx='196' cy='96' r='7'/><circle cx='212' cy='104' r='7'/><circle cx='228' cy='96' r='7'/><circle cx='220' cy='84' r='7'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Explain what ivory is (elephant tusk) and note sensitively that the ivory trade harmed elephants - a good link to Life Skills. The big idea: Africans here were not cut off from the world; they were active traders in a network reaching Asia. [SME] Please confirm the exact list of trade partners and ports for Grade 6 wording; sources mention China, India, Egypt and Arabia via East African coast ports.

slides.heading: The golden rhino
slides.lead: The best-known object from Mapungubwe is a small rhinoceros covered in gold.

blocks.text: Archaeologists found a tiny **rhinoceros made of thin gold** shaped over a wooden centre. It was found in a royal grave on top of the hill. The golden rhino shows how skilled Mapungubwe's goldsmiths were, and it is now a proud symbol of South Africa's deep history.
The golden rhino tells us that African people here were making beautiful gold art hundreds of years ago.

items: It was buried with a king or important leader.
Gold objects like this showed royal power and wealth.
South Africa's highest national honour is named the Order of Mapungubwe.

image.alt: A small golden rhinoceros figure with a single horn, standing on a base.

slides.notes: The golden rhino is the emotional high point of the lesson - a real object learners can picture. Keep it simple: thin gold foil over carved wood. [SME] Confirm which single figurine to foreground (the famous rhino) versus the wider gold collection, and the current museum home (University of Pretoria) if you want to name it. Avoid over-claiming exact weights at Grade 6 level.

slides.heading: Who lived where
slides.lead: Mapungubwe was one of the first places in the region where leaders lived apart from everyone else.

items: The king and royal family.
Kept apart to show they were special.
Buried with gold when they died.
Most ordinary people: farmers, herders and crafters.
Lived in round houses near their fields.
Buried without gold treasures.

slides.notes: This slide names the social structure directly. Use the words 'class' or 'rank' carefully for Grade 6: some people had more power and wealth than others, and you could see this in where they lived. Handle respectfully - avoid suggesting ordinary people were unimportant; they grew the food and made the goods that made the kingdom rich.

slides.heading: Rediscovering Mapungubwe
slides.lead: The story of Mapungubwe was told by the land itself - through what people dug up.

blocks.text: Local people always knew the hill was special. In **1932** a group led to the hilltop found stone walls, pottery, glass beads and gold. Since then, **archaeologists** (scientists who study the past by digging) have carefully uncovered graves, houses and objects that teach us how people lived.

items: We learn about Mapungubwe from objects, not from written books.
Beads and pottery show trade and daily life.
Graves show who was rich, powerful or ordinary.

image.alt: Hands and a brush uncovering pottery and beads buried in the ground.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='84' width='240' height='76' fill='#c8934a'/><path d='M60 128 q22 -22 48 0 Z' fill='#8a5a34'/><g fill='#3f6bb0'><circle cx='150' cy='120' r='5'/><circle cx='164' cy='126' r='5'/><circle cx='178' cy='120' r='5'/></g><rect x='150' y='60' width='40' height='8' rx='4' fill='#7a5a3a' transform='rotate(-20 170 64)'/><rect x='182' y='44' width='10' height='24' rx='3' fill='#d8c8a8' transform='rotate(-20 187 56)'/></svg>

slides.notes: Introduce the idea of archaeology and evidence - central to the CAPS focus on 'how we know about the past'. Note sensitively that although 1932 is called a 'discovery', people living nearby had long known the hill was sacred; the objects were new to scientists, not to local communities. [SME] Confirm the level of naming detail you want about the 1932 find for Grade 6.

slides.heading: The story of Mapungubwe

blocks.alt: A timeline from about 1100 to today showing Mapungubwe's rise, peak, decline and its later recognition.

events.label: People settle in the Limpopo Valley
Mapungubwe becomes a rich kingdom; king moves to the hilltop
Busy trade in gold and ivory with faraway lands
The kingdom declines and people move away
Gold and other objects found by archaeologists
Named a UNESCO World Heritage Site

slides.notes: Use the timeline to consolidate the sequence: rise, peak, decline, then rediscovery long after. Dates are approximate ('c.' means 'about') because there were no written records. Ask learners to work out roughly how many years the kingdom was powerful (about 80-100 years at its peak). [SME] Confirm the exact UNESCO listing year (commonly given as 2003).

slides.heading: Why did Mapungubwe decline?
slides.lead: After about 1300 the kingdom lost its power and most people left.

blocks.text: Around 1300 the weather seems to have become **colder and drier**. Less rain made it harder to grow crops and keep cattle for so many people in one place. Trade also began to move to a new centre, **Great Zimbabwe**, further north.

items: Drier weather made farming and herding difficult.
People moved away to find better land and water.
Great Zimbabwe grew into the next great trading kingdom.

image.alt: A cracked dry field with a hot sun and a bare tree, showing drought.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='196' cy='40' r='24' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect y='108' width='240' height='52' fill='#cbb27e'/><path d='M40 130 l20 -6 M100 140 l24 -4 M150 126 l26 6 M60 150 l30 -2' stroke='#9a8250' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/><path d='M60 108 v-30 q-14 -4 -18 -16 q16 6 18 8 q2 -14 -6 -22 q14 6 14 20 q6 -10 18 -12 q-8 12 -18 18 v34 Z' fill='#7a5a3a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Give both main reasons scholars discuss: climate change (drier, colder) and shifting trade to Great Zimbabwe. Be honest at Grade 6 level that we are not completely sure - this teaches that history sometimes has more than one possible explanation. Link forward: Great Zimbabwe is the next kingdom learners often study.

slides.heading: A proud African heritage
slides.lead: Mapungubwe reminds us that Africans built rich, clever kingdoms long ago.

blocks.text: For a long time some people wrongly said Africa had no great kingdoms of its own. Mapungubwe proves this wrong. Today it is a **World Heritage Site** protected for everyone, and South Africa's highest honour, the **Order of Mapungubwe**, is named after it.

items: It shows African skill in gold, farming and trade.
It is protected so future learners can study it too.
It gives South Africans a proud, shared history.

image.alt: A gold medal ribbon with a small rhino emblem, next to a protected-site shield.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M70 40 l18 40 h-36 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='70' cy='96' r='26' fill='#e8a13a' stroke='#c8871f' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M58 100 q6 -12 24 -8 q-4 8 -24 8 Z' fill='#a8762f'/><g><rect x='140' y='48' width='70' height='80' rx='6' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M175 60 l24 12 v18 q0 20 -24 30 q-24 -10 -24 -30 v-18 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='175' cy='92' r='9' fill='#7a8b5a'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: End the content on a note of pride and respect. Emphasise the anti-racist point gently for Grade 6: Mapungubwe is proof that people in southern Africa built advanced societies long before European settlement. Connect to heritage learners may know - World Heritage Sites, national honours.

slides.heading: What we learned
slides.lead: Mapungubwe was southern Africa's first kingdom - rich from farming, cattle and trade in gold and ivory, and ruled by a king on a hill.

blocks.text: Remember the big ideas: **where** (Limpopo Valley), **when** (about 1220-1300), **wealth** (gold and ivory trade), **the golden rhino**, the **king on the hilltop** above ordinary people, and the **decline** when the weather grew drier and trade moved to Great Zimbabwe.

blocks.question: Name two things Mapungubwe traded with faraway lands, and one thing it received in return.
Why did the king and royal family live on top of the hill? What did this show about them?
Give one reason historians think Mapungubwe declined after about 1300.

slides.notes: Use the three questions as a written task or class discussion. The mark allocations follow CAPS Intermediate Phase styles. Encourage learners to answer in full sentences and to use the lesson vocabulary: kingdom, trade, ivory, archaeologist, royal, decline. Close by linking to the next likely topic, Great Zimbabwe.
